title: Property Registration & Mutation
description: Step-by-step guide to register property and complete mutation of land/property records.

steps.details: Before purchasing, verify the property title through the Sub-Registrar office. Check the Encumbrance Certificate (EC) for the last 30 years to ensure no existing loans, mortgages, or disputes on the property.
Get a sale deed drafted by a lawyer. Both buyer and seller must sign the deed. The sale deed must include: property description, sale consideration, payment details, and delivery of possession.
Pay stamp duty as applicable in your state (typically 5-7% of property value). Stamp duty can be paid online or at authorised banks. Keep the stamp duty receipt.

Both buyer and seller must visit the Sub-Registrar office with 2 witnesses. Submit the sale deed, stamp duty receipt, and all documents. Pay the registration fee (typically 1% of property value). Biometric verification will be done.
steps.timeline: 1 day at office; registration completion in 7-15 days
documents_needed: Original sale deed with stamp duty
ID proof of buyer, seller, and 2 witnesses

steps.details: After registration, apply for mutation (name change) in the revenue/municipal records. Submit the registered sale deed to the Tehsildar (for land) or Municipal Corporation (for urban property). This transfers the property tax liability to your name.
Visit the local municipal office to update the property tax records in your name. Going forward, all property tax and utility bills should be in your name.

estimated_timeline: Registration: 7-15 days. Mutation: 15-45 days. Total: 1-2 months.
tips: ALWAYS get an Encumbrance Certificate before buying — it reveals hidden loans and disputes
Many states now offer online property registration — check your state's portal
Keep certified copies of all documents — originals should be kept in a bank locker
Under RERA, for new properties from builders, the builder must register the project first
If the seller refuses to execute the sale deed after agreement, you can file a suit for specific performance
For inherited property, you need a succession certificate or probate instead of a sale deed
